coredata.dc:title: Dependence of linear and non-linear optical properties to sp3 domains level and edges length in graphene-based nanomaterials
coredata.dc:creator: Afroozeh, Abdolkarim
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a single-layer of graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (r-GO), and graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanosheets were synthesized using a modified Hummers' method. AFM and FTIR spectroscopies were used to confirm single-layer graphene synthesis and proper reduction process. Photoluminescence (PL) and Z-scan characterizations showed a high existence of linear and non-linear optical properties in these nanomaterials. PL spectra of r-GO showed a peak at 665 nm, but an appeared blueshift in the PL spectrum of GO (600 nm) and GQD (500 nm) nanosheets indicate a bandgap opening in their band structures. According to Raman results, the I(D)/I(G) intensity ratio of GO is bigger than r-GO, which can be assigned to higher linear optical properties of GO than r-GO and higher non-linear optical properties of r-GO than GO. Finally, our results demonstrated that edges length and functional groups on the edges define the appearance of linear and non-linear optical properties in graphene-based nanomaterials, and these properties can be manipulatable in terms of oxidation/reduction level, surface area size and edge length.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Nanostructures
Graphene quantum dot
Linear and non-linear optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic picture of all the experimental procedures.
(a) AFM images of GO and (b) GQD samples, (c) height profile diagram of GO and (d) GQD nanosheets.
(a) TEM images of GO, (b) r-GO, (c) GQD samples.
(a) SEM images of the cross-section of GO and, (b) GQD samples.
XRD patterns of GO, r-GO, and GQD nanosheets.
Raman spectrum of GO, r-GO, and GQDs nanosheets at excitation wavelength of 785 nm.
FTIR spectra of GO, r-GO, and GQDs samples.
PL spectra of GO, r-GO and GQD nanosheets in aqueous phase at 500 nm excitation wavelength.

(a) Z-scan results of GO, r-GO, and GQD solutions, (b) Z-scan curves of GO-X (X = 6, 8, 10 and 12) and Y-rGO-6 (Y = 80, 100, 120, 140, 160 and 180) excited by an input intensity of 0.40 J/cm2 (Reprinted with permission from Wang et al. [33] Copyright 2017: Royal Society of Chemistry).

head.ce:title: Dependence of linear and non-linear optical properties to sp3 domains level and edges length in graphene-based nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Larestan, Lar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Larestan, Lar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a single-layer of graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (r-GO), and graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanosheets were synthesized using a modified Hummers' method. AFM and FTIR spectroscopies were used to confirm single-layer graphene synthesis and proper reduction process. Photoluminescence (PL) and Z-scan characterizations showed a high existence of linear and non-linear optical properties in these nanomaterials. PL spectra of r-GO showed a peak at 665 nm, but an appeared blueshift in the PL spectrum of GO (600 nm) and GQD (500 nm) nanosheets indicate a bandgap opening in their band structures. According to Raman results, the I(D)/I(G) intensity ratio of GO is bigger than r-GO, which can be assigned to higher linear optical properties of GO than r-GO and higher non-linear optical properties of r-GO than GO. Finally, our results demonstrated that edges length and functional groups on the edges define the appearance of linear and non-linear optical properties in graphene-based nanomaterials, and these properties can be manipulatable in terms of oxidation/reduction level, surface area size and edge length.

ce:section.ce:para: Tow last revolutionary decades of graphene revealed high potential usages of graphene-based nanomaterials in versatile fields such as biological [1], electrical [2], mechanical [3], and thermal [4] applications. These are due to unique electrochemical properties [5], mechanical specifications [6], low-cost production [7], easy functionalization [8], and large surface area [9]. However, lack of knowledge on linear and nonlinear optical properties of graphene-based nanomaterials and their adjusting ways has been made many challenges for using these materials in optical applications.

Graphene is a zero bandgap semiconductor, so optical properties in pure graphene is very unlikely. Nevertheless, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (r-GO), graphene quantum dot (GQD) could have a bandgap in their band structure. Furthermore, it was proposed that pure graphene could show remarkable nonlinear optical properties at THz frequencies [10]. π–π* interaction is introduced as a key factor for linear optical properties of GO and r-GO nanostructure, which is manipulatable in terms of oxidation and reduction level [11]. Electron–hole pairs arising from the edge effects are the main reason for optical property in GQD [12], these effects are ignorable in graphene with the large surface area but could make a significant impact comparing the small surface area of GQD.

In recent years, nonlinear optical properties of graphene-based nanomaterials have been attracted many interests due to the upcoming applications in ultrafast lasers [13] and optical sensors [14]. However, the origin of nonlinear optical properties of graphene family remained unknown. It is proposed that chirality and symmetry should have a significant role in the appearance of nonlinear optical properties in these materials [15]. Similar to the dependence of other superior properties of graphene-based materials to size, shape, number of layers, oxidation and reduction level, sp3/sp2 domains ratio, type of stacking, doping, and edge structure of graphene, both linear and nonlinear optical properties of graphene is not an exception from this rule. Size, shape (e.g. nanoribbons, nanobubbles, and nanoporous), and the number of layers in graphene-based nanostructure are key factors in determining the bandgap, so optical properties can be defined according to these factors [16–18]. During oxidation and reduction for GO and r-GO nanoflakes synthesis, oxidation and reduction levels may vary regarding utilized oxidizing and reducing substances, which can change sp3/sp2 domains ratio [19–21]. Besides, type of stacking (e.g. AA, AB, and ABC stacking) doping and edges structure (e.g. zigzag and armchair) can define bandgap, electrical conductivity, and optical properties [22–25].

In this current work, the GO nanoflakes were synthesized by using a modified hummers’ method. Then, GO nanoflakes were reduced via a mixture of DMAc/H2O and microwave treatment, and then grinded by KMnO4 and ultrasonication procedure. Finally, both linear and nonlinear optical properties of GO, r-GO, and GQD were studied using a PL spectroscopy and Z-scan technique.

Graphite oxide was synthesized by using a modified Hummers' method. First, 1 g graphite powder (TIMCAL) was oxidized with a mixture of 46 mL sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %) and 6 g KMnO4 at 0 °C in an ice bath while the mixture was stirred for 15 min (in this step, the color of the mixture was green). To ensure proper oxidation, the mixture was kipped on stirrer with 550 rpm for 5 h at 60 °C (at the end of this step, the color of the mixture was brown). After that, 100 mL DI water was added into the mixture at 35 °C and stirred at 700 rpm for 5 min. Finally, 100 mL DI water along with 6 mL H2O2 (30 %) was added until the color of suspension changed from chocolate brown to bright yellow. After 12 h and graphite oxide deposition, the residual acids were slowly removed from the mixture by a pipette. The obtained suspension was washed two times with 1:5 HCl (Merck, 30 %) solution in order to remove remaining metal ions by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 60 min. To obtain monolayer graphene oxide, the obtained sediment was dispersed into DI water and sonicated at a frequency of 40 kHz and power of 150 W for 15 min (60 C°). The achieved graphene oxide (GO) suspension was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove any graphite oxide and at 6000 rpm for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous monolayer GO suspension.

GO reduction was performed by using a mixture of N,N-dimethylacetamide and water (DMAc/H2O) along with microwave treatment. To do this, 100 mg GO powder was mixed with 30 mL water and 150 mL of DMAc under ultrasonication condition (60 C°). After almost 15 min, the prepared GO/DMAc/H2O suspension was put into a microwave oven under nitrogen gas at 800 W for 10 min. This procedure was repeated until the color of suspension turned to black. Finally, the obtained r-GO suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 60 min to remove DMAc compounds (for more details see [26]).

Firstly, 50 mg obtained r-GO were dispersed in 100 mL of water. Next, 300 mg KMnO4 was slowly added to the r-GO suspension, and ultrasonication was carried out at 150 W for 4 h (60 C°). Then, the obtained suspension was washed in one-step with 1:5 HCl (Merck, 30 %) solution in order to remove metal ions by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to separate GQDs nanosheets from r-GO nanosheets with large surface area (for more details see [27]). All the procedures from GO synthesis to fabrication of GQDs nanosheets are shown in Fig. 1 .

To study morphology and thickness of samples, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Park Scientific CR-Research model (VEECO)) was utilized at non-contact mode to confirm the preparation of monolayer GO and GQD nanosheets. Crystalline properties of the samples were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns obtained by using a D5000 model XRD-analyzer (SIEMENS, Germany). Morphology of the cross-section of GO and GQD films was investigated by using a Philips XL30 scanning electron microscope (SEM). The sheet-like morphology and transparency of the samples were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out in transmittance mode with a Perkin-Elmer RXI spectrometer to study functional groups on basal plane and edges of graphene surface and confirm proper reduction. A Raman spectrometer (Bruker Co. Model Senterra) was used to achieve Raman peaks of GO and r-GO samples and identify their specifications. Cary Eclipse fluorescent spectrometer was utilized to taken Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of aqueous GO, r-GO, and GQD samples. Non-linear optical property of GO, r-GO, and GQD nanoflakes was studied by using a Z-scan system consisting of an Nd:YAG laser emitting 10 ns pulses with a pulse repetition rate of 10 Hz at 532 nm. The thickness of the quartz sample cell for all the samples was 2 mm. the laser pulses are focused on the sample with a lens with a 200 mm focal length and W0 = 70 μm, so Rayleigh length is determined to be 30 mm.

The AFM images of GO and GQD nanosheets on mica substrates are shown in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. It can be seen that the lateral dimensions of the GO sheets are from ∼0.5 μm to ∼1 μm and the lateral dimensions of the GQD sheets are from ∼2 nm to ∼3 nm, which indicates the successful procedure of ultrasonication for GQD fabrication. The thickness of the GO and GQD nanoflakes is ∼1 nm [28,29], which demonstrate the preparation of monolayer GO and GQD nanosheets. The slight difference between GQD and GO nanosheets arises from functional groups on the basal plane of GO nanosheets.

An XRD spectrometer was used to provide information about the crystallographic formation of the GO, r-GO, and GQD samples. Fig. 5 shows the XRD patterns of the GO, r-GO, and GQD nanosheets. The diffraction peak of synthesized GO was appeared at 2θ ∼ 11.78°, indicating (002) crystalline orientation with ∼ 0.74 nm d-spacing. The diffraction peak for both r-GO and GQD nanosheets appeared at 2θ ∼ 25.2°, which showing the effectiveness of the reduction procedure and consequently a smaller d-spacing of ∼ 0.35 nm.

The main principle of Raman spectroscopy is phonon-photon interaction. The kind of phonon and their dominance depends on the lattice structure, the number of layers, and chemical groups of graphene-based nanomaterials, which define the intensity of various peaks in the Raman spectrum [30,31]. Fig. 6 shows a Raman spectrum of the GO and r-GO samples at 785 nm excitation wavelength. Distinct peaks could be identified from the Raman spectra of graphene-based nanomaterials. The intensity of D and G peaks appeared at ∼1300 and 1600 cm−1 which arises from the vibrations of sp3 (between carbon and functional groups) and sp2 (between two carbon atoms) bonding in carbon atoms, respectively. Hence, the D/G peak intensity ratio is known as a measure of the size of sp3/sp2 domains in GO sheets, so its amount can show oxidation and reduction levels of GO and r-GO [32]. According to Fig. 6 the I(D)/I(G) intensity ratio of GO, r-GO, and GQDs are 1.09, 0.92, and 0.88 respectively; which demonstrate the average size of the sp3 domains on the GO (r-GO and GQDs) surface is bigger (smaller) than the average size of the sp2 domains. The slight difference between the I(D)/I(G) intensity ratio of the r-GO and GQDs samples show that the GQDs fabrication process did not harmfully affect the chemical structure of r-GO. Besides, the sp3/sp2 ratio determines the bandgap in the GO and r-GO band structure. So, as much as the sp3/sp2 ration be bigger, the bandgap in the band structure of graphene material family reaches higher values.

FT-IR spectroscopy plays a key role in graphene research for distinguishing proper oxidation, reduction, and functionalization methods. According to Lerf-Klinowski model, carboxyl, epoxy, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups are the major functional groups on the basal plane and edges of GO [33]. FT-IR spectra of the GO, r-GO, and GQDs samples are shown in Fig. 7 . The OH, CO, and CO bonds of GO sample were appeared at ∼3400, 1630, and 1140 cm−1 peaks, respectively. For FT-IR spectra of the r-GO and GQDs samples, low intensity of ∼3400, 1630, and 1140 cm−1 peaks confirmed proper reduction of GO by DMAc/H2O mixture and microwave treatment. No significant difference between the FTIR spectrum of r-GO and GQDs samples showed that the GQD fabrication procedure did not affect the r-GO chemical structure.

The bandgap opening is very important for appearance photon-matter interaction. So, the sp3/sp2 ratio as band-gap measure considered as a key factor for linear and nonlinear optical properties of graphene-based materials. Fig. 8 shows the PL spectra of GO, r-GO, and GQD suspensions with the same concentration (0.1 mg/mL). PL spectra of the r-GO, GO and GQD showed a peak at 665, 600, and 500 nm, respectively. An appeared blueshift in PL spectra showed effectively of oxidation and size effect in the bandgap opening. The related bandgap of the r-GO, GO, and GQD nanosheets are 1.2, 1.3, and 1.6 eV, respectively. After the reduction of the GO, the intensity of the peak significantly reduced due to the eliminating of the π-π* bonding within functional groups and subsequent decrease of bandgap energy.

NLO properties of GO, r-GO, and GQD samples with 0.1 mg/mL concentration were measured using the Z-scan technique, while the laser power, irradiation time, input intensity, and pulse energy were fixed to 300 mW, 1 h, 3.4 × 1010 W/cm2, and 100 μJ. The optical limiting (OL) effect in graphene-based materials depends on various specifications, i.e. functional groups on basal plane and edges, edge effects, and type of stacking in multilayers graphene. The normalized nonlinear transmittances are shown in Fig. 9 (a). According to this figure, the intensity of NLO properties in various graphene nanosheets at the same conditions is GQD > r-GO > GO. Our results have a good fit with experimental and theoretical results of other authors in existing literature (Fig.9(b)) [34,35]. In GO, functional groups at the edge decrease NLO properties, but NLO properties increased after reduction. Since edge length to surface area ratio in GQD is higher than both GO and r-GO due to small size of GQD, so NLO properties of GQD expected to be higher than both GO and r-GO nanoflakes. In GO nanosheets, functional groups on the basal plane and the edges are considered as the linear optical source for wide-range photoluminescence of GO. Fluorescence property is associated with bandgap opening in GO, which can interpret from the sp3/sp2 domains ratio according to the Raman spectrum. After reduction, functional groups disappeared on r-GO and linear optical property lost its dominance. Furthermore, GQDs have a higher edge length/surface area ratio due to its small size, so the edge effect leads to higher NLO properties in these nanomaterials. Finally, our results showed that sp3 domains level and edges length of graphene-based nanomaterial have a significant impact on the appearance of linear and non-linear optical properties.

To define the estimation error of the Z-scan experiment, the measurement was repeated 5 times, the uncertainly of the measurement is ±10 %. The major error source comes from mistaking in determining sample cell displacement from the actual focal point [36] and also heat accumulation effect by laser pulses [37] which can change the chemical structure of the samples. It is well known that graphene is a good NIR absorbent, so the share of heat accumulation effect on the uncertainly is very smaller than the displacement error due to 532 nm wavelength of the Nd:YAG laser pulse.

In this recent work, a monolayer of GO, r-GO, and GQD with only ∼1 nm thickness were obtained using a modified Hummers' method. Material characterization were carried out via various characterization techniques i.e. AFM, TEM, SEM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, and PL spectroscopy. The Z-scan technique was utilized to study non-linear optical properties. The r-GO, GO, and GQDs suspensions showed a PL peak at ∼665, 600, and 500 nm, respectively. The appeared blueshift in the PL spectrum illustrates bandgap opening phenomena in terms of oxidation level and edge effect. So, graphene-based nanomaterial could show a wide range of fluorescence properties in terms of the sp3 domain level. The intensity of NLO properties in various graphene nanosheets at the same conditions is GQD > r-GO > GO. Our findings have a good fit with the results of other authors in the existing literature. Finally, our results suggest that sp3 domains in functional groups and edge length have a high impact on the appearance of both linear and non-linear optical properties. Additionally, these properties are adjustable in terms of oxidation/reduction level and edge size, which could be a practical advantage for using graphene-based nanomaterial in upcoming linear and non-linear optical applications. The study of both linear and nonlinear optical properties of graphene-based materials in presence of other functional groups or nanostructure must be considered in future works.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and characterization of graphene oxide for biological application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Maeh, Raghad Khwater, et al. "Preparation and characterization of graphene oxide for biological application." Drug Invention Today 14.5 (2020).
